content.heading: Putting it all together — your first real program

body.text: You've learned variables, numbers, strings, data types, input, comparisons, and conditionals. These aren't separate topics — they're ingredients. Real programs use all of them at once. This lesson is about mixing them together.
Here's a small program that combines everything from Session 2. Read through it carefully before moving to the exercises.

lines: // Variables to track the game state
let playerName = prompt('What is your name?');
console.log(`Keep going! You need ${50 - score} more points.`);

body.label: What you just used
body.text: That program used: variables (let), strings, numbers, template literals, prompt(), +=, --, comparison operators (===, >=), and conditionals (if/else if/else). That's the entire Session 2 toolkit.

exercises.title: Trace the full program
tasks: Read the starter code carefully. It simulates a full round of a quiz game — no running yet.
Trace through it on paper. Write down the value of score, lives, and correct after each section.
Predict the final console.log output.
Run it and check.
Now change the answer to make the player get it right. Trace through again.
exercises.hint: Start at the top. Follow every assignment, every operation, every condition — one line at a time.

exercises.title: Number guessing game
tasks: Set a secret number (pick any number 1-10 and store it in a variable).
Use prompt() to ask the player to guess a number between 1 and 10.
Convert the guess to a Number.
Use if/else if/else to respond:
- If correct: 'You got it! The number was [secret].'
- If the guess is too low: 'Too low! The number was [secret].'
- If the guess is too high: 'Too high! The number was [secret].'
Test with a correct guess, a low guess, and a high guess.
exercises.hint: Three conditions: guess === secret, guess < secret, and the else handles guess > secret.

checks.label: the secret number appears in the output

exercises.title: Grade calculator
tasks: Use prompt() to ask for a student's name.
Use prompt() to ask for their score (0-100). Convert to Number.
Use if/else if/else to assign a letter grade: A (90+), B (80-89), C (70-79), D (60-69), F (below 60).
Log: '[name] scored [score] and got a [grade].'
Challenge: also log whether they passed (grade is NOT 'F') using a boolean variable called passed.
exercises.hint: Build the grade string first (let grade = 'A'), then use it inside the template literal.

exercises.title: Build your own adventure
tasks: Create a tiny text adventure using everything you know.
Ask the player for their name.
Ask them to choose a path: 'forest' or 'cave'. (No conversion needed — this stays a string.)
If they pick forest: ask 'Do you have a torch? (yes/no)'
- If yes: 'You light the way and find treasure! You win!'
- If no: 'It's too dark to see. Game over.'
If they pick cave: ask 'How many potions do you have?'
- Convert to Number. If 2 or more: 'You survive the monster! You win!'
- If fewer than 2: 'You run out of potions. Game over.'
If they pick anything else: 'Unknown path. The game ends.',
Use template literals throughout.
exercises.hint: Nested if statements are fine — an if inside an if. Just keep track of your curly braces.

challenges.title: The win check fires too early
challenges.description: The player should win at 100 points. But they're winning at just 10 points. Find the bug.
challenges.hint: What should the winning threshold actually be?
challenges.explanation: The winning condition is score >= 10 but the win should be at 100. Also the conditions are out of order — the >= 10 catches everything above 10 before >= 50 gets a chance. Fix: change the first condition to score >= 100, and put the most specific condition (highest threshold) first.

challenges.title: The name length check crashes
challenges.description: This code should check if a username is long enough. But it sometimes gives a wrong answer. Find out why and fix it.
challenges.hint: Test with exactly 3 characters like 'abc'. Does it pass or fail? Is that the right behavior?
challenges.explanation: 'abc' has length 3. The condition is > 3, which means 3 characters would fail (3 is not greater than 3). That's actually correct behavior for 'more than 3'. But if the rule should be 'at least 3', change > to >=. This is about understanding what the rule actually needs to be.

challenges.title: The ticket buyer is underaged — but they got in
challenges.description: The event is 18+. The buyer said they're 17 but they still got a ticket. Find all the bugs.
challenges.hint: The age check is right. But why does ticketPrice even factor into whether someone is old enough?

checks.includes: Sorry, you must be 18 or older.
checks.label: rejects the 17-year-old buyer

challenges.explanation: ticketPrice is 20 and 20 <= 25 is true — so the || makes the whole condition true regardless of age. The ticket price has nothing to do with whether someone is old enough. Remove the || ticketPrice <= 25 entirely. The only check needed is buyerAge >= 18.
